ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for 'economic beehoon with fishcake and egg'; mapped to the closest parent class 'rice noodle dish' (FOODON_03315595) as an approximate match.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice noodles, cooked — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for this stir-fried rice vermicelli composite dish.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for economic beehoon with fishcake and egg; A00GH (rice noodles, cooked) is the closest defensible anchor for the primary carbohydrate component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No applicable SNOMED CT food concept identified for this composite hawker dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker plate of economic beehoon with fishcake and egg is approximately 300 g, comprising a base of stir-fried rice vermicelli topped with two to three slices of fishcake and one fried or scrambled egg.

nutrient_source.title: Economic Beehoon with Fishcake and Egg — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Economic Beehoon with Fishcake and Egg' (cr_id F05060052). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken verbatim from this entry. Saturated fat is estimated from HPB composite data for similar fried beehoon dishes where the exact entry does not separately report saturated fat.

gi_source.title: Rice noodles, cooked — Glycemic Index entry; analogous estimate applied to economic beehoon with fishcake and egg
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 'economic beehoon with fishcake and egg' in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI of 61 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked rice noodles (rice vermicelli), which is the dominant carbohydrate component of this dish. The addition of fishcake (protein and starch) and egg (protein and fat) is expected to modestly lower the effective GI relative to plain rice noodles; however, the rice noodle GI of 61 is used as a conservative upper-bound estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × (carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = 61 × (22.06 × 3.0) / 100 = 61 × 66.18 / 100 ≈ 40.4 g per 300 g plate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 505 mg per 100 g — significantly exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold, driven by processed fishcake and soy sauce seasoning.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150 g per meal, request reduced-sodium preparation, avoid adding soy sauce or chilli sauce, and substitute fishcake with fresh steamed fish to substantially reduce sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load (22.06 g/100 g) from rice vermicelli with low dietary fibre (0.75 g/100 g), yielding a medium-to-high glycaemic impact and a glycaemic load of approximately 40 g per standard 300 g plate.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce portion size to 150–200 g, pair with non-starchy vegetables to increase fibre and slow glucose absorption, and account for hidden starch in processed fishcake when calculating total carbohydrate intake for the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (505 mg/100 g) and moderate fat (4.39 g/100 g) from stir-frying oil and processed fishcake; saturated fat estimated at approximately 1.1 g/100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to small portions (150 g or less), request oil-reduced stir-frying, avoid processed fishcake in favour of fresh fish, and avoid adding sodium-rich condiments to keep total sodium per meal within the 600 mg target.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Economic Beehoon with Fishcake and Egg is a popular and affordable Singaporean hawker dish consisting of stir-fried rice vermicelli (beehoon) served with sliced processed fishcake and a fried or scrambled egg. The dish is a staple at school canteens, hawker centres, and coffee shops across Singapore, typically priced at SGD 1.50–2.50 per plate. The rice vermicelli base is stir-fried in a small amount of vegetable oil with light soy sauce and sometimes dark soy sauce, giving it a mild savoury flavour with a slightly chewy texture. Fishcake, a processed fish product made from surimi-style fish paste mixed with starch and salt, contributes protein and a firm, bouncy texture, but also adds significant sodium. The egg — usually fried sunny-side up or scrambled — adds richness and additional protein. Per 100 g, the dish provides 150 kcal, 5.4 g protein, 4.39 g fat, and 22.06 g carbohydrates, with a notably high sodium content of 505 mg. Dietary fibre is low at 0.75 g/100 g. The glycaemic index is estimated at 61 (medium) based on the rice vermicelli component, and the glycaemic load per standard 300 g plate is approximately 40 g — clinically significant for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is not suitable for individuals with hypertension due to its high sodium content, nor for those with Type 2 Diabetes without careful portion control. It is moderately concerning for cardiovascular health due to combined sodium and fat load. The dish is halal-certified in most hawker settings and is dairy-free, but contains fish, egg, and gluten allergens from soy sauce and fishcake starch.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05060052), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The match is exact. Saturated fat is estimated from HPB composite data for similar fried beehoon dishes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using cooked rice noodles as the closest approved proxy.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, full micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite hawker dish are not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for F05060052 as of April 2026; no supplementary breakdown was found in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for an equivalent composite dish. Saturated fat (1.1 g/100 g) is estimated from HPB composite data for similar stir-fried beehoon dishes and should be verified if a direct HPB breakdown becomes available. The input record incorrectly classified the cuisine as 'western'; this has been corrected to 'Singaporean' based on the dish's well-established origin and cultural context. The input record listed no portions; a standard 300 g plate has been applied based on typical hawker serving sizes documented in HPB portion guidance. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (cooked rice noodles); no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in the approved GI source as of April 2026.

q1_food_identity.notes: Canonical English name 'Economic Beehoon with Fishcake and Egg' is precise and unambiguous. All mandatory Singaporean local-language names are present: Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (150.0), protein_g (5.4), fat_g (4.39), and carbohydrate_g (22.06) are all present, numeric, and sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F05060052). nutrient_source is fully documented.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.75), sodium_mg (505.0), saturated_fat_g (1.1), and sugar_g (1.76) are all present as numeric values. Saturated fat is an HPB composite estimate, documented in notes.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (61), gi_category ('medium'), glycemic_load_per_serving (40.4), and gi_source are all present. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using cooked rice noodles as the approved proxy, with full documentation of the estimation basis.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn mapped to FOODON_03315595 (rice noodle dish) as a 'closest_parent' match with gap note. FoodEx2 mapped to A00GH (rice noodles, cooked) as a 'closest_parent' match with gap note. SNOMED CT is null with documented gap note. No codes are invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters (approximately 1,450 characters), covers food identity, preparation method, taste and texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients. common_user_queries and ingredient_synonyms are populated.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions are present: Hypertension (high concern), Type 2 Diabetes (high concern, human_condition_name correctly set), and Cardiovascular Disease (moderate concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and a specific clinical guidance sentence.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases array contains 13 entries covering English variants, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and romanized forms. aliases_by_language is fully populated and consistent with the flat aliases array. Self-check confirmed: all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases array.
